narrative_summary:
The MLH1 c.-42C>T substitution lies in the 5′‑untranslated promoter region of the mismatch‑repair gene and has been reported in families meeting Amsterdam criteria for hereditary non‑polyposis colorectal cancer (Lynch syndrome) [12919137].  The change disrupts a putative Myb transcription‑factor binding site and is therefore a candidate regulatory lesion.

Clinical observations show co‑segregation of the variant with colorectal cancer in a Newfoundland kindred, where six affected relatives had a mean age of onset of 62 years, later than typical HNPCC, and the variant has also been identified in isolated probands from Seattle, Germany and a Latino cohort, some of whom displayed loss of heterozygosity of the mutant allele in tumours [12919137, PMC3908650, PMC4682451, PMC5610604].  In the 1000 Genomes secondary‑findings study the variant was listed as a variant of uncertain significance, noting its detection in three affected individuals from the Newfoundland family and in single cases from other populations, but without a definitive clinical classification [PMC4558085].  A more recent analysis of constitutional MLH1 epimutations still categorises c.-42C>T as a VUS because its effect on promoter methylation is unclear, although reduced MLH1 expression was observed in carriers [PMC10239107].

Functional assays consistently demonstrate that the c.-42C>T allele markedly impairs transcription.  Electrophoretic mobility shift assays showed weaker nuclear protein binding to the mutant Myb site, and in vivo transfection of HeLa cells revealed only 37 % of wild‑type promoter activity [12919137].  Luciferase reporter experiments in HCT116 and HEK293 cells confirmed a major reduction in promoter‑driven expression for the c.-42C>T construct, comparable to the effect of another pathogenic SNV (c.-11C>T) and significantly greater than that of neutral deletions [PMC3908650, PMC4682451].

Population data indicate that the variant is extremely rare.  It was not identified among 304 healthy Australian controls screened for MLH1 promoter changes, and the 1000 Genomes dataset reports no minor alleles for this position, supporting its low frequency in the general population [PMC3908650, PMC4558085].  No computational predictive scores are provided in the extracted evidence.

Taken together, the variant shows strong functional impairment of MLH1 transcription and has been observed in multiple unrelated cancer cases with some evidence of segregation and tumour LOH, suggesting a deleterious effect consistent with Lynch‑syndrome predisposition.  However, formal clinical databases continue to list c.-42C>T as a variant of uncertain significance, reflecting limited segregation data and uncertainty about its role in inducing constitutional epimutations.  The balance of current evidence leans toward a likely pathogenic classification, but definitive pathogenicity remains unconfirmed pending larger familial studies and consensus re‑evaluation.

mentions.quoted_text: Hereditary non-polyposis colon cancer (HNPCC) is an autosomal dominant form of inherited predisposition to colorectal and other malignancies. It is associated with mutations in DNA mismatch-repair genes, especially hMSH2 and hMLH1. Management of HNPCC families is improved if the underlying mutation in each family can be discovered. We describe a Newfoundland kindred, meeting the Amsterdam Criteria for HNPCC, in which a mutation in the promoter region of the hMLH1 gene co-segregates with the disease phenotype. The **-42C > T** mutation is within a putative Myb proto-oncogene binding site. Using electrophoretic mobility shift assays, we demonstrated that the mutated Myb binding sequence is less effective in binding nuclear proteins than the wild-type promoter sequence. Using in vivo transfection experiments in HeLa cells, we further demonstrated that the mutated promoter has only 37% of the activity of the wild-type promoter in driving the expression of a reporter gene. The average age of onset in six family members affected with colorectal cancer is 62 years, which is substantially later than the typical age of onset in HNPCC families. This is consistent with a substantial decrease, but not total elimination, of mismatch repair function in affected members of this family. This is the first report of a heritable hMLH1 promoter mutation in any HNPCC family.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The MLH1 c.-42C>T promoter mutation co‑segregates with HNPCC and markedly reduces promoter activity, supporting its pathogenic role.
article_mentions.title: Germline hMLH1 promoter mutation in a Newfoundland HNPCC kindred.

mentions.quoted_text: Five probands (I-M, Table 2) from the population-based registry in Seattle had a sequence variant within the promoter or 5'UTR of MLH1 without any methylation (Table 2 and Figure 3). These comprised two SNVs, c.-11C>T and **c.-42C>T** and two novel microdeletions, delc.-411-413GAG and delc.-432-435AAAG. Because RNA was unavailable to directly assess whether the promoter variants caused a reduction in allelic expression in the subjects, luciferase promoter reporter assays were instead used to assess their effect on promoter activity (Figure 3b). The c.-11C>T and **c.-42C>T** SNVs showed a major reduction in promoter activity as compared with the wild-type promoter sequence, whereas the delc.-411-413GAG microdeletion showed a statistically significant but lesser degree of transcriptional reduction. These findings were consistent in transient transfections of both HCT116 and HEK293 cell lines. The delc.-432-435AAAG had no detrimental effect on promoter activity in these assays.

Sequence analysis of the entire MLH1 promoter revealed an additional four sequence variants in five index cases, none of which were associated with constitutional methylation. The luciferase assays demonstrated that the novel c.-11C>T SNV causes a profound constitutive downregulation of transcription, although one of the two novel microdeletions caused a minor reduction in transcriptional activity. Consistent with previous reports, the **c.-42C>T** SNV was associated with significantly reduced transcriptional output. Collectively, these findings suggest that some MLH1 promoter variants cause transcriptional dysregulation through a primarily DNA alteration-based mechanism; presumably by creating or destroying individual DNA-nucleoprotein binding motifs and thereby altering interactions with particular transcription factors. It is interesting to note that with the exception of one carrier of the c.-11C>T SNV, each of the other probands developed cancer at an older age of onset and did not have a remarkable family history of cancer, suggesting that the mechanism of inactivation of MLH1 may influence phenotype. Review of the MLH1 promoter sequences from 304 healthy Australians did not identify any of these sequence changes among them.

Pedigrees of individuals with sequence changes in the MLH1 promoter without constitutional methylation (families I-M) and their effect on promoter activity. (a) The family labels correspond with Table 2. The proband is shown by an arrow, squares are males, circles are females, and crosses indicate deceased individuals. The dark shading represents individuals with CRC, the gray shading is for other cancers. The sequence variant for each family is shown. Apart from one case (aged 34 years), the probands were over 50 years of age, all had right-sided CRC, and one person had multiple cancers including uterine cancer. (b) The promoter activity of a luciferase reporter construct containing the entire wild-type (blue) MLH1 promoter and 5'-UTR (from c.-513 to c.-1 with respect to the ATG start codon), and constructs containing the promoter sequence variants c.-11C>T, **c.-42C>T**, delc.-411-413GAG, and delc.-432-435AAAG (red) and also the SNPs c.-269C>G and c.-93G>A (yellow) in transient transfections of HCT116 and HEK293 cells are shown. The c.-11C>T and **c.-42C>T** SNVs each resulted in a major reduction in promoter activity as compared with the wild-type promoter sequence. The delc.-411-413GAG microdeletion showed a lesser degree of transcriptional reduction (77-89% of the wild type), although this was statistically significant, whereas the delc.-432-435AAAG appeared to have no detrimental effect on promoter activity in these assays. The c.-269C>G and c.-93G>A SNPs also demonstrated marginally diminished promoter activity specifically in HCT116 cells. Values are shown as the mean +- SD of the luciferase output normalized to a cotransfected Renilla control, and expressed as a percentage of the positive control vector, for multiple independent experiments performed in triplicate. CRC, colorectal cancer; SNP, single-nucleotide polymorphism; SNV, single-nucleotide variant.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study provides functional evidence that the MLH1 promoter variant c.-42C>T markedly reduces transcriptional activity, suggesting a pathogenic role in colorectal cancer predisposition.
article_mentions.title: Identification of constitutional MLH1 epimutations and promoter variants in colorectal cancer patients from the Colon Cancer Family Registry

mentions.quoted_text: Several class 3 variants within the MLH1 promoter have been found in cases of suspected Lynch syndrome, namely, c.-411_-413del, c.-432_-435del, c.-64G>T, c.-53G>T, **c.-42C>T**, c.-28A>G, c.-28A>T, c.-27C>A, c.-11C>T, and c.-7C>T [Green et al.,; Hitchins et al.,; Ward et al.,; Kwok et al.,; Thompson et al., ]. However, for most of these variants, it is unclear whether they abrogate MLH1 function directly or whether they are linked to another genetic defect within MLH1. Studies of the c.-27C>A variant provide the most compelling evidence that MLH1 promoter variants can directly affect the regulation of MLH1. This variant segregates with colorectal cancer in multiple cancer-affected families [Raevaara et al.,; Hitchins et al.,; Ward et al.,; Kwok et al., ] and has been associated with reduced transcriptional activity and the dominant inheritance of a mosaic constitutional MLH1 epimutation [Hitchins et al., ]. These studies suggest that MLH1 promoter variants may cause unbalanced constitutional expression of MLH1. In support of this, the c.-411_-413del, **c.-42C>T**, c.-27C>A, and c.-11C>T variants also significantly reduce the activity of the MLH1 promoter in driving the expression of a reporter gene [Green et al.,; Hitchins et al.,; Ward et al., ]. To date, however, no analysis of the potential pathogenicity of the c.-28A>G or c.-7C>T variants has been undertaken, despite the fact that these variants have been described in at least two unrelated individuals with suspected Lynch syndrome [Lee et al.,; Muller-Koch et al., ].

There are now several reports of Lynch syndrome associated with partial loss of MLH1 expression [Curia et al.,; Green et al.,; Hinrichsen et al., ]. Curia et al. described Lynch syndrome associated with a silent variant within exon 9 of the MLH1 gene and 50% reduction in constitutional levels of expression compared with the wild-type allele. Green et al. described a **c.-42C>T** variant within the MLH1 gene associated with the reduction of promoter activity to ~37% that of wild-type promoter sequence. Furthermore, several constitutional MLH1 missense mutations that lead to...

The variable age of onset of cancer associated with the c.-28A>G and c.-7C>T variants was evident from the two families we investigated. While Proband 32 presented with CRC aged 38 years, Proband N was asymptomatic at 54 years. However, the father and grandfather of Proband N presented with colonic adenomas and colorectal cancer at ages 50 and 40 years, respectively, though we were unable to ascertain whether the grandfather had the c.-28A>G and c.-7C>T variants. This variable penetrance may be explained by incomplete inactivation of MLH1 expression, as described previously for the **c.-42C>T** variant, which was associated with drastically different ages of cancer onset between 35 and 76 years (average 62 years) in a single Lynch syndrome family [Green et al., ]. Interestingly, in this study, loss of heterozygosity analyses of the tumor from an individual with the **c.-42C>T** variant revealed deletion of the MLH1 allele with the variant and not of the wild-type allele, as one would expect. In our analyses, both MLH1 alleles were retained in the tumor of Proband 32. These results are most likely explained by the presence of somatic MLH1 mutations in the tumor.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article presents functional reporter‑gene data and clinical segregation/LOH evidence indicating that the MLH1 c.-42C>T promoter variant reduces gene expression and is associated with Lynch‑syndrome‑related cancer, supporting its pathogenicity.
article_mentions.title: Lynch Syndrome Associated with Two MLH1 Promoter Variants and Allelic Imbalance of MLH1 Expression

mentions.quoted_text: ...a single-nucleotide substitution (-107C G) in the MLH1 promoter found in colorectal cancer populations has been shown to reduce transcriptional activity. The mutation **-42C > T** in the promoter region of the MLH1 gene, localized within a putative Myb protooncogene binding site, has been described in a Newfoundland kindred, meeting the Amsterdam Criteria for...

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article reports a rhesus macaque model of hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer linked to MLH1 promoter alterations and cites the human MLH1 c.-42C>T variant as observed in a Lynch‑syndrome family, suggesting its disease relevance.
article_mentions.title: MLH1-rheMac hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer syndrome in rhesus macaques

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study of Latino colorectal cancer patients identified MLH1 c.-42C>T in a tumor exhibiting loss of heterozygosity, within a cohort where 13% had mismatch‑repair‑deficient tumors and 5.7% were diagnosed with Lynch syndrome.
article_mentions.title: DNA mismatch repair deficiency and hereditary syndromes in Latino patients with colorectal cancer

mentions.quoted_text: To date, the MLH1: c.-27C > A germline pathogenic variant is the only reported variant known to underlie secondary MLH1 epimutations. Several other non-coding MLH1 promoter germline variants have been reported (e.g. c.-11C > T, c.-[28A > G; 7C > T], c.**-42C > T**), although their pathogenicity and effect on inducing MLH1 methylation are less defined and, as such, remain classified as variants of uncertain clinical significance (VUS). In CRCs associated with these VUS, a constitutional reduction of MLH1 expression was observed but without a clear effect on MLH1 promoter methylation. Due to their rarity, studies of these variants are scarce and validation difficult, which impedes optimal clinical management in carriers.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study shows that tumor methylation profiling and ultra-sensitive ddPCR can identify mosaic MLH1 epimutation carriers, but the c.-42C>T promoter variant is still considered a VUS with no clear effect on MLH1 methylation.
article_mentions.title: Identifying primary and secondary MLH1 epimutation carriers displaying low-level constitutional MLH1 methylation using droplet digital PCR and genome-wide DNA methylation profiling of colorectal cancers

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article reports the MLH1 c.-42C>T variant as a variant of uncertain significance, citing its detection in several cancer patients and functional assays showing reduced promoter activity, but no definitive pathogenic classification is provided.
article_mentions.title: Identification of Medically Actionable Secondary Findings in the 1000 Genomes
